title: The Big Picture: What Is Complex Analysis About?
description: Where I started: a complex function maps a plane to a plane, so an honest graph would need four dimensions. Domain coloring and grid deformation are the workaround pictures — every later lesson leans on them.

title: Complex Numbers, the Plane, and Basic Topology
description: The ground floor. Arithmetic and geometry of complex numbers, polar form, roots of unity, and just enough topology — open sets, boundaries, regions — to state the later theorems honestly.

title: Holomorphic Functions & the Cauchy-Riemann Equations
description: The first real theorem. What complex differentiability actually demands, the Cauchy-Riemann equations that fall out of it, and why passing that test forces a map to preserve angles.

title: Power Series & Elementary Functions
description: Power series take over: $e^z$ defined by its series, Euler's formula derived instead of assumed, and the logarithm turning multivalued — the first branch cut.

title: Cauchy's Theorem & the Cauchy Integral Formula
description: The centerpiece. Contour integrals, deformation invariance, Cauchy's theorem, and the integral formula: the values of a holomorphic function on a boundary determine every value inside.

title: Consequences of Cauchy's Formula
description: The payoff. One formula cascades into infinite differentiability, Cauchy's estimates, Liouville's theorem, and a proof of the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra with no algebra in it.

title: The Real-Analysis Bridge
description: The bridge. Five lessons in, the proofs had shifted from computing things to bounding things — so before series took over the subject, I paused to build the analysis underneath: sup and inf, rigorous limits, the triangle inequality, and the ML-estimate derived from scratch.

title: Power Series Representations & Analytic Continuation
description: The biggest structural fact yet: a holomorphic function equals its own Taylor series. That forces zeros to be isolated, gives at most one analytic continuation, and turns a singularity into something with an exact, measurable location — removable, pole, or essential.

title: Residues & Contour Integration
description: Of the infinitely many coefficients in a Laurent series, exactly one is worth computing for contour integration. That single coefficient becomes the Residue Theorem, and — via a semicircular contour and an ML-estimate — a genuine way to evaluate a real integral with no antiderivative in sight.
